content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 7, 1993


Charles H. Bell
Bell, McAndrews & Hiltachk
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 530
Sacramento, CA  95814

                                    Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
                                         Our File No. I-93-337

Dear Mr. Bell:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard-
ing the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹

     Please note, while the Act requires that the Commission
provide formal written advice to any person whose duties under the
Act are in question, or to that person's authorized
representative, the Commission will decline to provide advice to
third parties concerning another person's duties under the Act.
(Section 83114(b); Regulation 18329(b)(8).)

     Additionally, Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A) provides that the
Commission will decline to provide formal written advice with
respect to any conduct which has already taken place.

     However, since you have asked for general assistance
concerning the requirements of the Act, we can provide the
following general information.²




¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA  95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-93-337
Page 2


DISCUSSION

1. Are the conclusions in the Battersby Advice Letter, No.
A-91-034,3 and the Sprague Advice Letter, No. A-86-260 still
applicable in light of the decision in Hall v. City of Santa
Barbara (1992) 797 F.2d 1493?

Commission written advice is the application of the law to a
particular set of facts provided by the requestor. Thus, while
such advice may provide guidance to others regarding their
situations, they are not necessarily determinative.

You have asked whether the conclusions in the Battersby
Advice Letter, No. A-91-034, and the Sprague Advice Letter,
No. A-86-260, are still applicable. With respect to the facts of
those letters and those requestors, it does not appear that the
decision in Hall v. City of Santa Barbara, supra, would affect
those letters. Those letters concluded that a decision to impose
rent control on mobilehome lots would have a financial effect on
the mobilehomes that were located on those lots. This continues
to be the case.4

However, whether such conclusions are applicable to your
facts is a determination that the Commission will not make with
respect to third party requests or hypothetical inquiries. We
also note that you have provided numerous additional facts
pertaining to other issues, such as the "public generally"
exception. Further, it appears from the letter received from
David R. McEwen, City Attorney for the City of Lancaster, dated
September 16, 1993, that the facts contained in your letter are in
dispute. Thus, this letter should not be construed to evaluate
any of the factual information in your letter. The Commission
does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

2. In determining foreseeable and material financial effects
on a mobilehome, may the following be considered: (a) a four
county study of the effect of vacancy control provisions in
mobilehome rent control ordinances; (b) a current appraisal of the
specific property; (c) a mobilehome dealer's estimate in lieu of
an appraisal?

3 The Battersby Advice Letter, No. I-91-398, a follow up to the
earlier letter, did not specifically discuss vacancy control.

4 Hall v. City of Santa Barbara reached a similar conclusion,
that rent control ordinances without vacancy decontrol could grant
tenants benefit to the land. However, Commission advice is
limited to interpreting the provisions of the Act. Month-to-month
tenancies are exempted from the Act's definition of an "interest
in real property." (Regulation 18233.)

Our File No. I-93-337
Page 3


     As stated above, Commission advice is the application of the
law to a particular set of facts provided by the requestor.
(Regulation 18329.) The Commission does not act as the finder of
fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby, supra.)

     You have asked what type of evaluation of a mobilehome's
value may be relied upon in determining whether the mobilehome
will be materially affected by a decision.  While the Commission
cannot determine whether there will be a material financial effect
on an official's economic interest, we have advised that if it is
reasonable to rely on an appraisal or some other evaluation of
materiality that concludes that an effect would not be material,
the councilmember may participate in the decision.5

     Thus, so long as an official makes a good faith effort to
evaluate the materiality of a financial effect and concludes that
the effect will not be material, and the evaluation is reasonable
to rely upon, the official may participate.

     I hope this general information will be of assistance. If
you have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel
free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.6

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: John W. Wallace
                                            Counsel, Legal Division




_____________________________
5   For example, in the Stone Advice Letter, No. A-92-133a, we
advised that if the person performing an appraisal was qualified
to determine the values of real property and determined, based on
the Commission's materiality standards, that decisions would have
no material financial effect on the official's real property
interests, the determinations and the official's reliance would be
considered a good faith effort to assess the materiality of the
pending decisions on the official's real property interests.

6   Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.  Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.question:
1. Are the conclusions in the Battersby Advice Letter, No. A—91—034, and the Sprague Advice Letter, No. A-86-260 still applicable in light of the decision in Hall v. City of Santa Barbara (1992) 797 F.2d 1493?

2. In determining foreseeable and material financial effects on a mobilehome, may the following be considered: (a) a four county study of the effect of vacancy control provisions in mobilehome rent control ordinances; (b) a current appraisal of the specific property; (c) a mobilehome dealer's estimate in lieu of an appraisal?
sections.conclusion:
With respect to the facts of those letters and those requestors, it does not appear that the decision in Hall v. City of Santa Barbara, supra, would affect those letters. Those letters concluded that a decision to impose rent control on mobilehome lots would have a financial effect on the mobilehomes that were located on those lots. This continues to be the case. However, whether such conclusions are applicable to your facts is a determination that the Commission will not make with respect to third party requests or hypothetical inquiries.

As stated above, Commission advice is the application of the law to a particular set of facts provided by the requestor. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. You have asked what type of evaluation of a mobilehome's value may be relied upon in determining whether the mobilehome will be materially affected by a decision. While the Commission cannot determine whether there will be a material financial effect on an official's economic interest, we have advised that if it is reasonable to rely on an appraisal or some other evaluation of materiality that concludes that an effect would not be material, the councilmember may participate in the decision. Thus, so long as an official makes a good faith effort to evaluate the materiality of a financial effect and concludes that the effect will not be material, and the evaluation is reasonable to rely upon, the official may participate.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether prior FPPC advice letters regarding rent control's financial effects on mobilehomes remain applicable after Hall v. City of Santa Barbara, and what methods may be used to evaluate whether a mobilehome will experience material financial effects from a decision.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The prior advice letters' conclusions regarding rent control's financial effects on mobilehomes remain applicable to their original facts. The Commission cannot determine materiality as a factual matter but will accept reasonable appraisals or evaluations if an official makes a good faith effort to assess materiality based on the Commission's standards.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document is characterized as 'informal assistance' rather than formal written advice. The FPPC explicitly declines to answer the third-party request regarding applicability to requestor's specific facts, limiting response to general information. Two discrete questions are presented in the Discussion section.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Are the conclusions in the Battersby Advice Letter, No. A—91—034, and the Sprague Advice Letter, No. A-86-260 still applicable in light of the decision in Hall v. City of Santa Barbara (1992) 797 F.2d 1493?

2. In determining foreseeable and material financial effects on a mobilehome, may the following be considered: (a) a four county study of the effect of vacancy control provisions in mobilehome rent control ordinances; (b) a current appraisal of the specific property; (c) a mobilehome dealer's estimate in lieu of an appraisal?

CONCLUSION: With respect to the facts of those letters and those requestors, it does not appear that the decision in Hall v. City of Santa Barbara, supra, would affect those letters. Those letters concluded that a decision to impose rent control on mobilehome lots would have a financial effect on the mobilehomes that were located on those lots. This continues to be the case. However, whether such conclusions are applicable to your facts is a determination that the Commission will not make with respect to third party requests or hypothetical inquiries.

As stated above, Commission advice is the application of the law to a particular set of facts provided by the requestor. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. You have asked what type of evaluation of a mobilehome's value may be relied upon in determining whether the mobilehome will be materially affected by a decision. While the Commission cannot determine whether there will be a material financial effect on an official's economic interest, we have advised that if it is reasonable to rely on an appraisal or some other evaluation of materiality that concludes that an effect would not be material, the councilmember may participate in the decision. Thus, so long as an official makes a good faith effort to evaluate the materiality of a financial effect and concludes that the effect will not be material, and the evaluation is reasonable to rely upon, the official may participate.

embedding.summary: This FPPC correspondence declines to provide third-party advice or evaluate disputed facts, but provides general guidance that prior advice letters on rent control and mobilehome financial effects remain valid, and that officials may rely on reasonable appraisals to determine whether financial effects are material.
